scraped.title: JIO STAR INDIA PVT LTD VS. IPTV SMARTER PRO & ORS

timeline.detail: The court allowed the plaintiff's application to implead additional rogue websites and their domain name registrars as defendants, subject to existing interim orders. The amended memo of parties was directed to be taken on record, and summons were ordered to be issued via email to the newly added defendants upon filing of process fee.
timeline.orderText: $~15 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 108/2025 JIO STAR INDIA PVT LTD .....Plaintiff Through: Ms. Ishi Singh, Adv. (through VC) versus IPTV SMARTER PRO & ORS .....Defendant Through: Ms. Mehek Thappa & Mr. Avish Sharma, Advs. for D-1 (through VC) Ms. Anmol Dhindsa & Mr. Yogesh Singh, Advs. for D-7. Mr. Ishakaran Singh Bhandari (CGSC) & Mr. Piyush Yadav, Advs. for METY & DOT (UOI) (through VC) (M- 9729927381) Ms. Harshitha Rathod, Adv. for D-45. CORAM: JOINT REGISTRAR (JUDICIAL), SUDHIR KUMAR SIROHI (DHJS) O R D E R % 07.05.2026 List the matter for the purpose fixed on the date already fixed i.e. 26.05.2026. IA No. 12869/2026 (By plaintiff u/O I Rule 10 CPC seeking impleadment of newly identified associated domains/URLs/UIs engaged in infringing activities as domains/URLs/UIs) & extension of ad-interim order dated 10.02.2025) The plaintiff is seeking to implead additional rogue websites This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 12/05/2026 at 16:31:56 & their domain name registrars as the defendant nos. 100 to 103 since they are illegally distributing the copyrighted content of the plaintiff. The relevant details of these infringers are mentioned in para no.5 of the application. Keeping in view, the contents of this application so read in the light of the prayers made in the suit, the same is allowed. It is worthwhile to note that the additional rogue websites & their domain name registrars impleaded as the defendant nos. 100 to 103 shall be subject to the interim orders already passed in the suit. The amended memo of parties be taken on record. Summons be issued to the defendant nos. 100 to 103 through all permissible modes, subject to the filing of PF. At this stage, ld. counsel for the plaintiff requests to issue summons through email only as the newly identified defendants are rogue websites, therefore the summons be issued via emails, subject to the filing of PF. intelligence.reliefGranted: Impleadment of additional rogue websites and their domain name registrars as defendant nos. 100 to 103, subject to existing interim orders.
directions: Amended memo of parties be taken on record.
Summons be issued to defendant nos. 100 to 103 through email, subject to filing of process fee.

timeline.detail: The court granted real-time blocking relief to the plaintiff against rogue websites and mobile applications infringing its broadcast rights, particularly for upcoming sporting events like IPL 2025 and England Tour of India 2025. The relief was limited until 03.07.2025, and the court relied on previous orders in similar cases.
timeline.orderText: $~43 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 108/2025 STAR INDIA PVT LTD .....Plaintiff Through: Mr. Sidharth Chopra, Mr. Yatinder Garg, Ms. Disha Sharma and Ms. Rimjhim Tiwari and Ms. Isha Singh, Advs. versus IPTV SMARTER PRO & ORS. .....Defendants Through: Mr. Avish Sharma, Adv. for D-1, 4 & 36. Ms. Mrinal Ojha, Mr. Debarshi Dutta, Mr. Arjun Mookerjee, Ms. Nikita Rathi and Mr. Nikhil Gupta, Advs. for D-7. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SAURABH BANERJEE O R D E R % 29.05.2025 I.A. 14129/2025 (for Stay) 1. The plaintiff filed the captioned suit for permanent injunction restraining the illegal and unlawful activities by defendant nos.1 to 4 and other unknown defendants (rogue websites) from infringement of copyright and broadcast reproduction rights. 2. In an accompanying application, vide order dated 10.02.2025 this Court had granted ex parte ad interim injunction to the plaintiff, whereby the said rogue websites as also their respective Domain Name Registrars (DNRs) were directed to lock and suspend all associated domains/ URLs/ UIs, as also to the Internet Service Providers (ISPs) to suspend access to This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:00 the same. Additionally, the plaintiff was also granted the liberty to implead newly identified domains/ URLs/ UIs, as and when discovered during the course of the proceedings. 3. Pursuant thereto, the plaintiff has been filing as many as seven impleadment applications for bringing on record three new mobile application (rogue mobile applications) and 16 new rogue domains/ URLs/ UIs engaged in illegally broadcasting the plaintiff’s content without authorisation. 4. Vide the present application, the plaintiff seeks further directions to facilitate timely and effective action against newly impleaded rogue websites as well as rogue mobile applications and their associated domains/ URLs/ UIs that are infringing the plaintiff’s exclusive content, and to enable real-time blocking of such platforms in relation to the ongoing and the upcoming sporting events, such as the Indian Premier League 2025, as also the forthcoming England Tour of India 2025 scheduled to commence during the vacation from 01.06.2025 to 30.06.2025, amongst others. 5. In support of the above, learned counsel appearing for the plaintiff submits that the procedural limitation of approaching this Court repeatedly on discovery of new websites and rogue mobile applications, especially in view of the upcoming vacation from 01.06.2025 to 30.06.2025, poses a serious risk of substantial and irreparable harm to the plaintiff especially in view of the imminent and high-value sporting events as aforementioned. Learned counsel hence prays that a case for grant of real- time blocking relief against rogue websites and rogue mobile applications that may be discovered has been made out by the plaintiff. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:00 6. For the aforesaid, learned counsel for the plaintiff places reliance on the order passed by this Court in a separate suit bearing no.CS(COMM) 266/2025 entitled Star India Private Limited v. https//crichdplayer.org & Ors. wherein a real-time injunction was granted qua the Indian Premier League 2025, albeit with respect to rogue websites and not rogue mobile applications extending to their associated domains/ URLs/ UIs. 7. Learned counsel further places reliance on the case bearing no.CS (COMM) 688/2023 entitled Star India Pvt. Ltd. & Anr. v. Jiolive.Tv and Ors. wherein this Court emphasised the necessity of blocking rogue websites on a real-time basis in connection with sporting events, amongst numerous other orders passed by this Court wherein real-time relief has been granted. 8. In view thereof, learned counsel lastly submits that the prayers sought in the present application may be restricted only till 03.07.2025 and not any further. 9. Learned counsel for the defendant no.7 opposes the grant of the aforesaid relief(s), particularly, since the relief(s) sought for by the plaintiff in the present application are beyond the reliefs sought for in the present suit. 10. This Court has heard learned counsel for the parties as also gone through the pleadings and documents on record. 11. The plaintiff is the true owner of the exclusive rights in respect of its content, either originally made or through broadcasting rights acquired by it, and has also obtained an ex parte ad interim injunction order dated 10.02.2025 from this Court. There is also no doubt that the rogue websites, as also the rogue mobile application and their associated This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:00 domains/ URLs/ UIs are operating without any bona fide, with a predominant intention of infringing content on their interfaces and an organised nature of infringement, with complete disregard for the intellectual property rights of the plaintiff. 12. In the new age of technology, today it has become increasingly easy and convenient for infringers to create alpha-numeric/ mirror/ redirect variants of infringing websites, and by the time impleadment and extension of relief(s) can take place, certain time-sensitive infringing activities like live streaming of sporting events have already commenced illegally and by the time the effected party like the plaintiff approaches this Court, it is too late. Even otherwise, the post blocking scenario is also a race against time since new alpha-numeric/ mirror/ redirect variants of infringing websites spawn

intelligence.reliefGranted: Real-time blocking relief against rogue websites and rogue mobile applications for sporting events until 03.07.2025
directions: Grant real-time blocking of newly discovered rogue websites and mobile applications infringing plaintiff's content.
Direct Domain Name Registrars to lock and suspend associated domains/URLs/UIs.
Direct Internet Service Providers to suspend access to infringing platforms.
Relief limited to period up to 03.07.2025.

timeline.detail: The court impleaded Televizo App as defendant no. 36 and hybridtv.live as defendant no. 39 in a copyright infringement suit. Televizo's counsel argued it is merely a client-side video player not involved in distribution. The court clarified that the interim orders apply to defendant no. 39 but not to defendant no. 36. Summons were issued and the matter was listed for 01.07.2025.
timeline.orderText: $~21 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 108/2025 STAR INDIA PVT LTD .....Plaintiff Through: Mr. Yatinder Garg, Ms. Rimjhim Tiwari, Ms. Ishi Singh & Ms. Disha Sharma, Advs. versus IPTV SMARTER PRO & ORS. .....Defendant Through: Mr. Mrinal Ojha, Mr. Debarshi Dutta, Mr. Yogesh Singh & Ms. Nikita Rathi, Advs. for D-7. CORAM: SIDHARTH MATHUR (DHJS), JOINT REGISTRAR (JUDICIAL) O R D E R % 01.05.2025 In view of its impleadment ordered today, issue summons to the defendant no. 36. This defendant has accepted the summons through counsel today in the court and the plaintiff shall now supply the copy of the plaint and the paper-book to its counsel, via email whereafter the written statement may be filed in accordance with law. In continuation of today’s impleadment, issue summons to the defendant no. 39 through all permissible modes, subject to the filing of PF. List the matter for the completion of service/pleadings on the date already fixed i.e. 01.07.2025. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:00 IA No. 10861/2025 (By plaintiff u/s 151 CPC seeking an early hearing in the IA No. 10055/2025 in CS(COMM) 108/2025) Issue notice. Accepted. The counsel for the proposed defendant no. 36 states that an early hearing may be permitted. Accordingly this application is allowed, whereby an early hearing of the IA No. 10055/2025 is being conducted today itself. IA No. 10055/2025 (By plaintiff u/o 1 Rule 10 CPC seeking impleadment of newly identified application & Its associated domains engaged in infringing activities as original defendants (associated rogue domains/URLs/UIs) & extension of ad-interim order dated 10.02.2025) The plaintiff is seeking to implead the newly identified IPTV App i.e. Televizo as well as the impugned URL i.e. hybridtv.live on the ground that the copyrighted content of the plaintiff is being televised/distributed through them. The counsel for the Televizo App states that they are not involved in any distribution of the copyrighted content of the plaintiff, because they are merely a client side video player like VLC whereupon anyone and everyone can play their content without the active approval/knowledge/consent/permission of its developer/owner. He thus states that the impugned URL of hybridtv.live is being operated from this App without the active approval/knowledge/consent/involvement of anyone associated with Televizo. The counsel for the plaintiff states that the impugned broadcast, via hybridtv.live needs to be stopped and for that appropriate directions qua the concerned ISP & DNR must be issued This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:00 so as to prevent the illegal distribution of the copyrighted content of the plaintiff. In view of the aforesaid submissions and counter-submissions, I am of the view that the App named Televizo & the impugned URL i.e. hybridtv.live, may be impleaded as separate defendants instead of a single entity as prayed in the application, so that no adverse effect is caused to Televizo App or its operation wherein the distribution of the copyrighted content of the plaintiff is not involved. Accordingly the “Televizo” App IPTV player is impleaded as defendant no. 36, while the impugned URL “hybridtv.live” is separately impleaded as defendant no. 39. The application is accordingly disposed off. It is worthwhile to note that the additional mirror/re-direct/alphanumeric variation/URL of defendant no. 39 i.e. “hybridtv.live” shall be subject to the interim orders already passed in the suit and thus the concerned ISP & DNR shall do the needful for the implementation of those interim orders qua the defendant no. 39. It is made clear that this order has no bearing qua defendant no. 36 as far as the removal of the impugned URL of “hybridtv.live” is concerned. The amended memo of parties be now filed in due course. (M): 9999064036 Email: yatinder@saikrishnaassociates.com versus IPTV SMARTER PRO & ORS. .....Defendants Through: Mr. Avish Sharma with Mr. Subhashish Kumar, Mr. Himesh Dwivedi and Ms. Ishika Jain, Advocates for defendant no. 1. (M): 8603705017 Email: subhashish.adv@gmail.com Mr. Mrinal Ojha with Mr. Debarshi Dutta, Mr. Arjun Mookerjee and Ms. Nikita Rathi, Advocates for defendant no. 7. (M): 9674388460 Email: arjun.mookerjee@solarislegal.in CORAM: HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE MINI PUSHKARNA O R D E R % 10.02.2025 1. The present is an application under Section 151 of the Code of Civil I.A. 3365/2025 (Exemption from filing original and certified copies of documents) This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:01 CS(COMM) 108/2025 Page 2 of 10 Procedure, 1908 (“CPC”), on behalf of the plaintiff, seeking exemption from filing original documents/certified copies of documents/clearer copies of documents/documents with appropriate margins/translated copies and seeking permission to file dim documents. 2. Exemption is granted, subject to all just exceptions. 3. Plaintiff shall file legible, clear, and translated copies of the documents, on which the plaintiff may seek to place reliance, before the next date of hearing. 4. Accordingly, the present application is disposed of. 5. The present is an application under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, read with Section 151 of CPC, seeking exemption from undergoing Pre-Institution Mediation. I.A. 3364/2025 (Exemption from undergoing Pre-Institution Mediation) 6. Issue notice to the defendants. 7. Notice is accepted by learned counsels appearing for defendant nos. 1 and 7. 8. Let notice be issued to other defendants. 9. This is an application under Order XI Rule 1(4) read with Section 151 CPC, as amended by the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, seeking leave to file additional documents. I.A. 3367/2025 (Application seeking leave to file additional documents) 10. The plaintiff, if it wishes to file additional documents at a later stage, shall do so strictly as per the provisions of Commercial Courts Act, 2015 and the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018. 11. The application is disposed of, with the aforesaid directions. I.A. 3366/2025 (Exemption from advance service to the defendant nos. This is a digitally signed order. The present is an application under Section 80(2), read with Section 151 CPC, seeking exemption from advance notice to the defendant nos. 10 (BSNL), 13 (MTNL), 17 [Department of Telecommunication (DoT)] and 18 [Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MEITY)]. 13. Considering the submissions made before this Court and considering the urgent nature of the suit, exemption is allowed. 14. With the aforesaid directions, the present application is disposed of. 15. Let the plaint be registered as suit. CS(COMM) 108/2025 16. Issue summons. 17. Summons is accepted by learned counsels appearing for defendant nos. 1 and 7. Let written statement be filed within thirty days from today by the said defendants. 18. Upon filing of the Process Fee, issue summons to the other defendants by all permissible modes. 19. Let the written statement be filed by the other defendants within thirty days from the date of receipt of summons. Along with the written statement, the defendants shall also file affidavit of admission/denial of the plaintiff’s documents, without which, the written statement shall not be taken on record. 20. Liberty is given to the plaintiff to file replication within thirty days from the date of receipt of the written statement. Further, along with the replication, if any, filed by the plaintiff, an affidavit of admission/denial of documents of the defendants, be filed by the plaintiff, without which, the replication shall not be taken on record. If any of the parties wish to seek This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. List before the Joint Registrar (Judicial) for marking of exhibits, on 14 th 22. List before the Court on 14 April, 2025. th July, 2025. 23. The present suit has been filed for permanent injunction restraining the defendant nos. 1 to 4 alleging that they are engaged in the infringement of plaintiff’s copyrights and broadcast reproduction rights, rendition of accounts, damages, etc. I.A. 3363/2025 (Application under Order XXXIX Rules 1 and 2 CPC) 24. Learned counsel appearing for the plaintiff submits that the plaintiff is a leading entertainment and media company in India, engaged, inter alia, in the production of popular content broadcast on its STAR channels. The plaintiff is also an exclusive right owner for several work broadcasted on its STAR channels. Plaintiff, along with its affiliates, is the owner of an extensive portfolio of more than 100 channels in over ten languages – including channels like Star Movies, Colors Infinity, Sports18, Star Bharat, Star Gold, Jalsha Movies, Star Sports 1, Star Sports HD 1, Star Sports Select 1, Star Sports Select HD 1, Maa Movies, St

timeline.detail: The Joint Registrar closed the right of defendants 1, 2, 4-15 to file written statements as they were served but did not respond. The court issued notices on two IAs filed by the plaintiff: one for impleading defendants 17-19 and another for impleading rogue domains/URLs (defendants 20-24) and extending an ad-interim order. Proposed defendant 24 accepted notice; others to be served via email. The matter is listed for further proceedings on 24.04.2026.

timeline.detail: The court exempted defendant nos. 3, 14, and 15 from further appearance after compliance with a prior order. Defendant no. 13 was deleted from the case due to inability to comply with directions. Plaintiff was granted liberty to implead a financial institution for UPI details. The matter was adjourned for service and pleadings.
timeline.orderText: Page 1 of 2 $~22 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 331/2025 & I.A. 9306/2025 STAR INDIA PVT LTD ......Plaintiff Through: Ms. Disha Sharma and Ms. Ishi Singh, Advs. versus DEVRAJ & ORS. ......Defendants Through: Ms. Swati Agarwal, Mr. Shashank Mishra, Mr. Vaarish Sawlani and Ms. Rithika Mathur, Advs. for D-3 Mr. Mayank Mikhail Mukherjee Adv. for D-13 (Through VC) Ms. Radhika B. Dubey, Mr. Gurleen Kaur W., Mr. Kritarth. Upadhyay & Mr. Saksham Sharma, Advs. for D-14 & 15 CORAM: HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE MANMEET PRITAM SINGH ARORA O R D E R % 26.08.2025 1.Learned counsel for defendant no. 3 submits that defendant no. 3 has complied with order dated 09.04.2025 and filed the compliance affidavit on record. 2.She prays that defendant no. 3 may be exempted from further appearance and in case, any further direction is sought against defendant no.3, the plaintiff can move an appropriate application and service can be affected through the counsel, who has already entered appearance. 3.Learned counsel for defendant nos. 14 and 15 joins the aforesaid This is a digitally signed order. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:13 Page 2 of 2 submissions of the defendant no. 3 and requests that defendant nos. 14 and 15 may also be exempted from further appearance. 4.Learned counsel for the plaintiff have no objection to the aforesaid prayer of defendant nos. 3, 14 and 15. 5.Accordingly, Defendant nos. 3, 14 and 15 are exempted from further appearance in view of their statement vis-à-vis compliance of order dated 09.04.2025. 6.Learned counsel appearing for defendant no. 13 submits that the directions issued to the said defendant at paragraph 33(e) cannot be complied with by the said defendant as this information is not available with this entity. 7.The statement of the learned counsel for defendant no. 13 is taken on record and in view of the said submission, defendant no. 13 is deleted from the array of parties. 8.Plaintiff is granted liberty to implead the appropriate financial institution/bank, which would have the particulars sought for the UPI id enlisted in Document D of the plaintiff. 9.At request of the plaintiff, list before the learned Joint Registrar (J) for completion of service and pleadings on 13.10.2025, the date already fixed. timeline.detail: The court disposed of several interim applications in a copyright infringement suit. It exempted the plaintiff from pre-litigation mediation and from advance service on certain defendants (BSNL, MTNL, DoT, MEITY). The suit was registered and summons issued to all defendants, with directions for filing written statements and replications.
timeline.orderText: CS(COMM) 331/2025 Page 1 of 12 $~41 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 331/2025 STAR INDIA PVT LTD .....Plaintiff Through: Mr. Sidharth Chopra, Mr. Yatinder Garg, Ms. Disha Sharma, Ms. Ishi Singh and Ms. Rimjhim Tiwari, Advs versus DEVRAJ & ORS. .....Defendants Through: CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SAURABH BANERJEE O R D E R % 09.04.2025 1. Vide the present application under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, read with Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (CPC), the plaintiff seeks exemption from pre-litigation mediation. I.A. 9307/2025-Exp from pre institution mediation 2. Considering the averments made in the present application, as also since the plaintiff is seeking ex parte ad interim injunction, and in view of the order passed by the Hon’ble Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Krithi 2024 (5) SCC 815, which has been followed by a Division Bench this Court in Chandra Kishore Chaurasia v. R. A. Perfumery Works Private Limited 2022:DHC:4454-DB, the plaintiff is exempted from instituting pre-litigation mediation. 3. Accordingly, the present application stands disposed of. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:14 CS(COMM) 331/2025 Page 2 of 12 4. Exemption allowed, subject to all just exceptions. I.A. 9308/2025-Exp 5. The application stands disposed of. 6. Vide the present application under Section 80, read with Section 151 of the CPC, the plaintiff is seeking exemption from the requirement of advance service upon the defendant nos. 5, 8, 14 and 15, i.e., Bharat Sanchar Nigam Ltd. (BSNL), Mahanagar Telephone Nigam Ltd. (MTNL), the Department of Telecommunications (DoT), and the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MEITY) respectively, on the ground that no formal remedy/ relief as prescribed under the Copyright Act, 1957 (the Act), is being claimed against them and since urgent reliefs are also sought against other defendants in other accompanying applications, as also since they are only being arrayed to ensure compliance with any orders that may be passed by this Court. I.A. 9309/2025-Exempt the plaintiff from issuing notice to D-5, 8, 14 & 15 7. For the reasons stated in the present application, as also taking into account the aforesaid connected factors involv ed, it would be in the interest of justice if the plaintiff is granted exemption from effecting advance service upon the aforesaid defendant nos. 5, 8, 14 and 15, i.e., BSNL, MTNL, DoT, and MEITY respectively. 8. Accordingly, the present application stands disposed of. 9. Vide the present application under Order XI Rule 1(4) read with Section 151 of the CPC, the plaintiff seeks leave of this Court to file additional documents. I.A. 9310/2025-Additional documents This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:14 CS(COMM) 331/2025 Page 3 of 12 10. The plaintiff will be at liberty to file additional documents at a later stage, albeit, after initiating appropriate steps, strictly as per the provisions of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 read with Section 151 of the CPC and the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018. 11. Accordingly, the present application stands disposed of. 12. The plaintiff vide the present plaint seeks permanent injunction and damages for infringement of copyright under the Act, as also appropriate directions to the arrayed authorities. CS(COMM) 331/2025 13. Let the plaint be registered as a suit. 14. Upon filing of the process fee, issue summons of the suit to the defendants through all permissible modes, returnable before the learned Joint Registrar on 23.07.2025. 15. Needless to say, it shall be specified in the summons that the written statement(s) shall be filed by each of the defendants within a period of thirty days from the date of the receipt of the summons. Written statement(s) shall be accompanied by affidavit(s) of admission/ denial of documents of the plaintiff, without which the written statement(s) shall not be taken on record. 16. Replication(s) thereto, if any, be filed by the plaintiff within a period of fifteen days from the date of receipt of written statement(s). The said replication(s), if any, shall also be accompanied by affidavit(s) of admission/ denial of documents filed by the defendants, without which the replication(s) shall not be taken on record within the aforesaid period of fifteen days. 17. If, and when, any of the parties wish to seek inspection of any This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:14 CS(COMM) 331/2025 Page 4 of 12 document(s), the same shall be sought and given within the requisite timelines. 18. List before the learned Joint Registrar for marking exhibits of documents on 23.07.2025. It is made clear that if any party unjustifiably denies any document(s), then such party would be liable to be burdened with costs. 19. Vide the present application under Order XXXIX, Rules 1 and 2 of the CPC the plaintiff is seeking the following reliefs: I.A. 9306/2025-Stay “i. Pass an order of ex parte interim injunction restraining the Defendant No.1 (and such other associated URLs/domains which are discovered during the course of the proceedings and notified on Affidavit by the Plaintiff), their owners, partners, proprietors, officers, servants, affiliates, employees, and all others in capacity of principal or agent acting for and on its behalf, or anyone claiming through, by or under it, from in any manner communicating to the public, hosting, storing, reproducing, streaming, broadca

timeline.detail: The Joint Registrar recorded compliance by some defendants and directed the plaintiff to file amended memo of parties, compliance affidavit under Order 39 Rule 3 CPC, and process fee for newly impleaded defendants. The matter was adjourned for completion of pleadings.

timeline.orderText: $~24 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 89/2026 JIOSTAR INDIA PRIVATE LIMITED .....Plaintiff Through: Ms. Ishi Singh, Adv. versus GHD SPORTS & ORS. .....Defendant Through: Ms. Anmol Dhindsha and Mr. Yogesh Singh, Advs for proposed D-152. CORAM: JOINT REGISTRAR (JUDICIAL) MS. SWATI KATIYAR O R D E R % 15.04.2026 Fresh I.A. 10205/2026 on behalf of the plaintiffs seeking impleadment of additional applications/associated domains/urls/uis and their domain name registrars engaged in infringing activities as the defendant nos. 22-158. 1. The plaintiff is seeking to implead additional rogue-websites and their domain name registrars as defendant nos. 22 to 158 because the websites have been found to be indulging in illegally disseminating the copyrighted material belonging to the plaintiff. 2. Proposed defendant nos. 22 to 150 are rogue websites and the defendant no.151 to 158 are DNR of proposed defendants. The application is supported by the required affidavit. Keeping in view the contents of the suit and the application, the same is allowed. 3. Ld counsel for plaintiff submits that they are not pressing for reliefs qua proposed defendant no.48 and 155 and they would like This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:57:26 to drop them from the array of the parties. In view of submissions made by Ld counsel for plaintiff, proposed defendant no.48 and 155 stands deleted from the array of parties. 4. Let fresh amended memo of parties be filed within one week. Steps be taken to serve the newly impleaded defendants (defendant nos.22 to 47, 49 to 154 and 156 to 158) with summons within two weeks, through the electronic mode only, on filing of PF. 5. The order blocking the rogue-websites and injunction order passed by the Hon’ble Court on 30.01.2026 shall operate qua the freshly impleaded defendants as well. Further, the concerned DNR’s are directed to lock and suspend / deactivate the rogue websites impleaded today. The concerned ISPs /TSPs are directed to block the rogue websites impleaded today. The concerned DNR’s are also directed to disclose complete details of the new rogue websites in an affidavit within four weeks, to be filed in the Court. Plaintiff shall comply with Order 39 Rule 3 CPC and file compliance affidavit within a week. IA is allowed and disposed off accordingly. MS. JUSTICE JYOTI SINGH O R D E R % 30.01.2026 I.A. 2588/2026 (for pre-institution mediation) 1. This application is filed on behalf of the Plaintiff under Section 12-A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 read with Section 151 of CPC seeking exemption from Pre-Institution Mediation. 2. Having regard to the facts of the present case wherein urgent relief is prayed for and in light of the judgment of Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Keerthi, (2024) 5 SCC 815, as also Division Bench of this Court in Chandra Kishore Chaurasia v. RA Perfumery Works Private Ltd., 2022 SCC OnLine Del 3529, exemption is granted to the Plaintiff from Pre-Institution Mediation. 3. Application is allowed and disposed of. I.A. 2589/2026 (u/S 80 r/w Section 151 CPC) 4. This application is filed on behalf of the Plaintiff under Order XXXIX Rules 1 and 2 read with Section 151 of CPC for grant of ex parte ad interim injunction for restraining Defendants No. 1 to 4 and other unknown parties from infringing Plaintiff’s Broadcast Reproduction Rights. 20. Issue notice to the Defendants through all permissible modes, returnable before Court on 07.04.2026. 21. It is the case of the Plaintiff as averred in the plaint that Plaintiff is a leading entertainment and media company in India engaged inter alia in production of popular content broadcasted on its channels and owns exclusive rights in several works broadcasted on its channels. Plaintiff owns the media rights in respect of various sporting events including international cricket matches organized by the International Cricket Council (‘ICC’) and Board of Control for Cricket in India (‘BCCI’). Plaintiff along with its affiliates is the owner of an extensive portfolio of more than 100 channels in over ten languages (‘JioStar Channels’) and also owns and operates OTT platform, JioHotstar. 22. It is stated that Defendants No. 5 to 9 are Domain Name Registrars (‘DNRs’), where the said associated UI(s)/URL(s)/Website(s) are being hosted. It is stated that Plaintiff has acquired inter alia media rights, including television rights (to be exercised via broadcast delivery systems), digital rights (to be exercised via digital delivery systems, including through internet and mobile technology) and certain ancillary rights for various ICC events, including ongoing ‘ICC Under 19 Men’s Cricket World Cup 2026’ (‘Ongoing Event’) and the upcoming ‘ICC Men’s T20 Cricket World Cup 2026’ (‘Upcoming Event’), organized under the aegis of ICC [collectively referred to as ‘Events’]. timeline.detail: The court disposed of several applications in a suit by Jiostar India Private Limited seeking to restrain unauthorized broadcast of TATA IPL 2026. Exemption from pre-institution mediation was granted due to urgent interim relief. Exemption from notice to certain government defendants was allowed. Plaintiff was permitted to file additional documents within 30 days. The application for interim injunction was not decided on merits.

timeline.detail: The court allowed the plaintiff's application to implead 66 additional defendants (nos. 21-86) in a copyright and broadcast infringement suit. It also granted an ex-parte injunction against defendant nos. 21-66 restraining them from infringing the plaintiff's exclusive content, and directed defendant nos. 67-86 to suspend domain registrations and disable access. Typographical errors in the applications were corrected on oral request.
timeline.orderText: CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 1 of 12 $~46 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 756/2025 JIOSTAR INDIA PRIVATE LIMITED .....Plaintiff Through: Mr. Yatinder Garg, Ms. Disha Sharma, Mr. Priyansh Kohli and Ms. Ishi Singh, Advs. versus HTTPS//IPTVWORLD.IN & ORS. .....Defendants Through: Mr. Shashank Mishra and Mr. Vaarish K. Sawlani, Adv. for D-6 Ms. Amee Rana, Ms. Sana Banyal and Mr. Debditya Saha, Advs. for D- 86 CORAM: HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE MANMEET PRITAM SINGH ARORA O R D E R % 15.09.2025 I.A. 22885/2025 (seeking impleadment of the necessary defendants) 1.This is an application filed by the plaintiff under Order I Rule 10 Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (‘CPC’) seeking impleadment of defendant nos. 21 to 86. 2.At the outset, learned counsel for the applicant/plaintiff states that, though the averments in the application pertaining to the proposed defendant Nos. 21 to 86 are correct, a typographical error has inadvertently crept into prayer clauses (b), (c), and (d). 2.1He states that on his oral request, the prayer clauses be corrected to read as under: This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 2 of 12 “... b.Implead the Domain name Registrars of the additional associated domain URLs/ UIs, i.e., Defendant Nos. 67 – 84, to the instant suit; c.Implead Binance Holdings Limited as Defendant No. 85; d.Implead Meta Platforms Inc. as Defendant No. 86; ” 3.It is noticed that the error is in reference to the defendant number. The same is corrected and has been highlighted in bold. The oral prayer of the plaintiff is accepted and the prayer shall read as above. 4.Learned counsel for the plaintiff submits that the proposed defendant nos. 21 to 66 are additional associated domain/URLs/UIs; proposed defendant nos. 67 to 84 are Domain Name Registrants (‘DNRs’) of the said additional domains; proposed defendant no. 85 is Binance Holding limited; and proposed defendant no. 86 is Meta Platforms Inc. 5.In view of the averments made in the application, the Court is satisfied that proposed defendant nos. 21 to 66 are necessary parties and defendant nos. 67 to 85 are proper parties for adjudication of the reliefs sought in the present suit; so as to stop the continuing infringing activities by the defendants. Accordingly, the application is allowed and defendant nos. 21 to 86 are hereby impleaded. 6.Amended memo of parties, filed along with this application, be taken on record. 7.Learned counsel for the plaintiff states that he will move an appropriate application for amending the plaint for incorporating necessary averments qua defendant nos. 21 to 86 within a period of one (1) week. This statement is taken on record. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 3 of 12 8.With the aforesaid directions, the application is disposed of. I.A. 22889/2025 (under Order XXXIX Rules 1 & 2 CPC) 9.This is an application filed by the plaintiff under Order XXXIX Rules 1 and 2 CPC seeking injunction against defendant nos. 21 to 66 from infringing copyright and broadcast reproduction rights of the Plaintiff by unlawfully providing access to the Plaintiff's exclusive content, including live channels and films, without authorization, and seeking directions to defendant nos. 67 to 86 as well as defendant nos. 6, 8 to 18. 10.Learned counsel for the applicant/plaintiff, at the outset, states that typographical errors have crept in the prayer clauses of the captioned application. 10.1He states that on his oral request, the prayer clauses be corrected. He states that the defendant number has been mentioned incorrectly at prayer clause (ii), (iii), (vii) and (viii) to read as under: “ i.Pass an order of ex-parte injunction restraining the Defendant Nos. 21 to 66, their owners, partners, proprietors, officers, servants, affiliates, employees, and all others in capacity of principal or agent acting for and on its behalf, or anyone claiming through, by or under it, from in any manner communicating to the public, hosting, storing, reproducing, streaming, broadcasting, re-broadcasting, causing to be seen or heard by public on payment of charge and/or making available for viewing the Plaintiff’s Exclusive Content through any app / domains / Rogue Domains, amounting to infringement of the Plaintiff’s copyright and broadcast reproduction rights; ii.Pass an order of injunction directing the Defendant Nos. 67 to 84, its directors, officers, affiliates, servants, employees, and all others in capacity of principal or agent acting for and, on their behalf, or anyone claiming through, by or under it, to suspend the domain name registration of the websites identified at Document ‘A’; This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 4 of 12 iii.Pass an order of injunction directing the Defendant No. 67 to 84, its directors, officers, affiliates, servants, employees, and all others in capacity of principal or agent acting for and, on their behalf, or anyone claiming through, by or under it, to disclose the following information of the Additional Defendant Domains/UIs (Defendant Nos. 21 to 66) as identified at Document ‘A’: a)Complete details (such as Name, Address, Email address, Phone Number, IP Address etc.); b)Mode of payment along with payment details used for registration of domain name by the Registrant. iv.Pass an order directing Defendant No.6, WhatsApp LLC, their directors, partners, proprietors, officers, affiliates, servants, employee

intelligence.summary: The court allowed the plaintiff's application to implead 66 additional defendants (nos. 21-86) in a copyright and broadcast infringement suit. It also granted an ex-parte injunction against defendant nos. 21-66 restraining them from infringing the plaintiff's exclusive content, and directed defendant nos. 67-86 to suspend domain registrations and disable access. timeline.detail: The court disposed of the suit against Defendant No. 1 based on a joint settlement application under Order XXIII Rule 3 CPC. The settlement agreement was modified to remove the plaintiff's right to revive monetary claims upon breach, while preserving criminal remedies. A decree for permanent injunction was passed in terms of the plaint's prayer clause 78(i).

JUSTICE MANMEET PRITAM SINGH ARORA O R D E R % 12.08.2025 I.A. 19638/2025 (joint application under Order XXIII Rule 3 r/w Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 filed by the plaintiff and defendant no. 1) 1. The present application has been filed by the Plaintiff and Defendant no. 1 under Order XXIII Rule 3 of Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (‘CPC’) for recording the terms of settlement arrived between the parties vide Settlement Agreement dated 08.08.2025 and seeking a decree based on the said settlement. 2. The Settlement Agreement is annexed as document ‘A’ of the captioned application. 3. The Plaintiff’s authorized representative and Defendant no. 1 are personally present in Court. 4. Learned counsel for the Plaintiff states that Plaintiff is seeking a decree in terms of the prayer clause mentioned at paragraph 78(i) of the suit against Defendant no. 1. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 2 of 4 4.1 He states that in view of the terms and conditions set out in the Settlement Agreement, the Plaintiff is not pressing its relief for damages and other consequential reliefs against Defendant no. 1. 4.2 He prays that the suit may be decreed qua Defendant No. 1 in terms of prayer clause paragraph 78(i) of the plaint and in terms of Settlement Agreement dated 08.08.2025. 5. Mr. Shashi Kant Jha, Advocate on behalf of Defendant no. 1 submits that the agreement has been signed by Defendant no. 1 after due negotiations and understanding of the terms and conditions set out therein. 6. Defendant no. 1, who is present in Court submits that he attended college for the course of B.Com, though he could not graduate. He states that he understands English language and he has signed this agreement after understanding the terms and conditions set out therein. 7. This Court has heard the learned counsel for the Plaintiff and the Defendant no. 1 and has perused the terms and conditions of the agreement. 8. The condition at clause ‘7’ of the Settlement Agreement, stating that Plaintiff shall be entitled to revive its claims (monetary claims) against the Defendant No. 1, is not tenable as the suit is being finally disposed of qua Defendant No. 1. The original clause 7 reads as under: - “7. That in the event of any breach of the terms of this Settlement Agreement by the Defendant No. I, including but not limited to breach of the injunction, failure to cooperate in the investigation, or any false or misleading disclosure, the Plaintiff shall be entitled to take appropriate legal action including revival of claims against the Defendant, pressing of criminal charges including those arising from FIR No. 0279, and pursuing damages as originally claimed in the suit. All rights and remedies of the Plaintiff in such event shall stand expressly reserved. “ This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:19 CS(COMM) 756/2025 Page 3 of 4 9. Learned counsel for the Plaintiff states, on instructions from the Plaintiff’s representative, that Plaintiff withdraws its right to initiate revival of monetary claims against the Defendant no. 1 in case of breach of the Settlement Agreement; however, the Plaintiff reserves its right to press criminal charges including those arising from FIR No. 0279. This statement of the Plaintiff is taken on record and it is bound down to the same. 10. The reference to the Settlement Agreement in this order will be read with the modification of clause 7 to the extent referred to in paragraph nos. 8 and 9 of this order. The modified clause 7 shall read as under: - 7. That in the event of any breach of the terms of this Settlement Agreement by the Defendant No. I, including but not limited to breach of the injunction, failure to cooperate in the investigation, or any false or misleading disclosure, the Plaintiff shall be entitled to take appropriate legal action including revival of claims against the Defendant, pressing of criminal charges including those arising from FIR No. 0279. All rights and remedies of the Plaintiff in such event shall stand expressly reserved. 8. This Court has perused the Settlement Agreement entered between the Plaintiff and Defendant no. 1, annexed as document ‘A’ in the captioned application, and is satisfied that the said settlement (as modified above) satisfies the requirements of Order XXIII Rule 3 CPC. The settlement is lawful and therefore, there is no impediment in decreeing the suit in terms of the settlement entered between the parties. 9. The statements and undertakings given by the parties are accepted by this Court and the parties are held bound by the same. 10. Consequently, the application is allowed, and the captioned suit is decreed qua Defendant no. 1 in terms of prayer clause 78(i) of the suit as well as the (modified) Settlement Agreement. timeline.detail: The court allowed the plaintiff's application for exemption from pre-litigation mediation and from issuing notices to certain defendants. It also granted an ex-parte ad-interim injunction against the defendants for copyright infringement, with Defendant No. 1 agreeing to take down the infringing website and share information on other rogue URLs. The court directed the domain name registrar to take down a specific server domain.

Learned counsel for Defendant No. 1 states that he has already e-filed his vakalatnama. He is directed to provide a copy to the counsel for the Plaintiff. 13. He states that IPTV login server details provided to the customer are http://junoon.fun:8080. 14.1. In response, learned counsel for the Defendant no. 1 states that https://iptvworld.in is a run through server domain of http://junoon.fun:8080. 14.2. He states that Defendant no. 1 has no objection to the server domain http://junoon.fun:8080 being taken down. He states however Defendant no. 1 has no control over this domain name. He states that, in fact, in view of the submission of Defendant No. 1 this website also has to be taken down. 15. The statement of Defendant No. 1 is taken on record. intelligence.summary: The court allowed two applications (I.A. 29478/2025 and I.A. 29479/2025) seeking impleadment of additional rogue websites (defendant nos. 835-887 and 888-942) in a copyright infringement suit. The existing dynamic injunction and blocking orders from 08.08.2025 were extended to the newly impleaded defendants. Directions were issued for service, suspension of websites, and disclosure of details by domain name registrars.

timeline.detail: The court allowed two applications (I.A. Nos. 28425/2025 and 28422/2025) to implead additional rogue websites (defendant nos. 730-781 and 782-834) in a copyright infringement suit. The dynamic injunction from the order dated 08.08.2025 was extended to these new defendants, and directions were issued to DNRs and ISPs to block and suspend the infringing websites.
timeline.orderText: $~35 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 810/2025 JIOSTAR INDIA PRIVATE LIMITED .....Plaintiff Through: Ms. Ishi Singh (VC), Advocate Versus SERIALMAZA.MY & ORS. .....Defendant Through: Ms. Manisha Agrawal Narain, CGSC, Ms. Aditi Singh GP for UOI Mr. Vignesh Raj T, Advocate for D-40, 140 CORAM: JOINT REGISTRAR (JUDICIAL) Dr. AJAY GULATI O R D E R % 14.11.2025 I.A. No. 28425/2025 on behalf of the plaintiff seeking impleadment of additional websites and their domain name registrars engaged in infringing activities as defendant nos. 730-781 (rouge websites) in the memo of parties. 1. The plaintiff is seeking to implead additional rogue- websites as defendant nos. 730 to 780 because they have been found to be indulging in illegally disseminating the copyrighted material belonging to the plaintiff. The application is supported by the required affidavit. Proposed defendant no. 781 is a DNR of proposed defendant no. 776 whose presence is also necessary This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:32 to give effect to the dynamic injunction issued by the Hon’ble Court. Keeping in view the contents of the suit and the application, the same is allowed. The proposed defendants are impleaded as defendant nos. 730 to 781. Ld. Counsel for the plaintiff is also directed to serve a copy of this IA on the defendant DNR’s who are already in the array i.e. defendant nos. 37, 38, 40, 41, 42, 45, 139, 140, 144, 314, 382, 447, 560 and 561 since these DNR’s are concerned with some of the rogue websites which have been impleaded today. 2. The amended memo filed alongwith this IA is taken on record. Steps be taken to serve the newly impleaded defendants with summons within two weeks, through the electronic mode only, on filing of PF. 3. The order blocking the rogue-websites passed by the Hon’ble Court on 08.08.2025 shall operate qua the freshly impleaded defendants as well, in terms of directions contained in para 36 of the order dated 08.08.2025. The injunction order passed by the Hon’ble Court contained in para 35 (a) of the order dated 08.08.2025 shall operate qua the newly impleaded rogue websites. Further, in terms of para 35 (b) and (c), the concerned DNR’s who are already in the array and the one impleaded today, are directed to lock and suspend / deactivate the rogue websites impleaded today. The concerned DNR’s are also directed to disclose complete details of the new rogue websites in an affidavit within four weeks, to be filed in the Court. Defendant nos. 47 to 55 / ISP’s and defendant nos. 56 and 57 are directed to take necessary steps qua defendant nos. 730 to 780, as were given by the Hon’ble Court in paras 35 (d) and (e) of the order dated 08.08.2025. IA is allowed and disposed off accordingly. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. No. 23943/2025 on behalf of the plaintiff seeking impleadment of additional websites and their domain name registrars engaged in infringing activities as defendant nos. 146 to 218 (rouge websites) in the memo of parties. 1. The plaintiff is seeking to implead additional rogue- websites as defendant nos. 146 to 216 because they have been found to be indulging in illegally disseminating the copyrighted material belonging to the plaintiff. Proposed defendant nos. 217 & 218 are DNR’s concerning 2 of these rogue websites which are sought to be impleaded. The application is supported by the required affidavit. Keeping in view the contents of the suit and the application, the same is allowed. The proposed defendants are impleaded as defendant nos. 146 to 218. Ld. The order blocking the rogue-websites passed by the Hon’ble Court on 08.08.2025 shall operate qua the freshly impleaded defendants as well, in terms of directions contained in para 36 of the order dated 08.08.2025. timeline.detail: The Joint Registrar considered an application for interim relief and noted that process fees for service of notice and summons were under objections. The plaintiff was granted liberty to file fresh process fees within two weeks. Compliance by defendant nos. 40 and 140 was noted, and they were directed to file an affidavit of compliance. The matter was adjourned to 08.01.2026.

JUSTICE MANMEET PRITAM SINGH ARORA O R D E R % 08.08.2025 I.A. 19338/2025 (seeking exemption from pre-institution mediation) 1. This is an application under Section 12A(1) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 [‘Act of 2015’] read with Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 [‘CPC’], filed by the Plaintiff seeking exemption from instituting pre-litigation mediation. 2. Having regard to the fact that the present suit contemplates urgent interim relief and in light of the judgement of the Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Keerthi 1 , exemption from the requirement of pre- institution mediation is granted to the plaintiff. 3. Accordingly, the application stands disposed of. I.A. 19339/2025 4. This is an application filed under Order XI Rule 1(4) of CPC [as amended by the Act of 2015] read with Section 151 CPC seeking leave to file additional documents. 8. The Plaintiff, if it wishes to file additional documents at a later stage, shall do so strictly as per the provisions of the Commercial Courts Act and the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018 [‘DHC Rules’]. 9. For the reasons stated in the application, the same is allowed. 10. Accordingly, the application is disposed of. CS(COMM) 810/2025 11. Let the plaint be registered as a suit. 12. Issue summons to Defendant nos. 1 to 35. In the peculiar facts of this case, summons be issued by the registry to the said Defendants through e- mode on filing of process fee. Affidavit of service(s) be filed within two (2) weeks. 13. Since Defendant no. 36 to 57 have been impleaded only for seeking compliances of directions issued qua Defendant nos. 1 to 35, no summons are being issued to the said Defendant nos. 36 to 57 at this stage. Notice in I.A. 19337/2025 has been issued to said defendants. 14. Defendant No. 58 is stated to be John Doe/Ashok Kumar and therefore issuance of summons to the said defendants is deferred. 15. The Plaintiff is at liberty to file replication(s) thereto within thirty (30) days after filing of the written statement(s). The replication(s) shall be accompanied by affidavit(s) of admission/denial in respect of the documents filed by Defendant nos. 1 to 35, failing which the replication(s) shall not be taken on record. 17. The parties shall file all original documents in support of their respective claims along with their respective pleadings. In case parties are placing reliance on a document, which is not in their power and possession, its detail and source shall be mentioned in the list of reliance, which shall also be filed with the pleadings. 18. It is made clear that any unjustified denial of documents may lead to an order of costs against the concerned party. 19. Any party seeking inspection of documents may do so in accordance with the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018. 20. List before the learned Joint Registrar (J) for completion of service and pleadings on 18.09.2025. 21. List before the Court on 20.01.2026. I.A. 19337/2025 (Under Order XXXIX Rule 1 and 2 CPC) 22. timeline.detail: The court disposed of several applications filed by the plaintiff, including exemptions from advance service and pre-institution mediation, permission to file additional documents and evidence on a pen drive. The suit was registered and summons issued to the defendant. The application for ex parte ad interim injunction was not decided; notice was issued to the defendant returnable on 30.04.2026.
timeline.orderText: CS(COMM) 195/2026 Page 1 of 23 $~51 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 195/2026 RELIANCE CONSUMER PRODUCTS LIMITED .....Plaintiff Through: Mr. Sidharth Chopra, Mr. Yatinder Garg, Ms. Disha Sharma, Mr. Akshay Maloo, Mr. Ishi Singh and Mr. Shudhata Sudhir, Advocates. versus RIPTON BEVERAGES PRIVATE LIMITED .....Defendant Through: CORAM: HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE JYOTI SINGH O R D E R % 26.02.2026 I.A. 5415/2026 (u/S 151 CPC) 1. This application is filed on behalf of the Plaintiff seeking exemption from effecting advance service on the Defendant. 2. For the reasons stated in the application, the same is allowed exempting Plaintiff from effecting advance service on the Defendant. 3. Application stands disposed of. I.A. 5416/2026 (for pre-institution mediation) 4. This application is filed on behalf of the Plaintiff under Section 12-A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 seeking exemption from Pre-Institution Mediation. 5. Having regard to the facts of the present case wherein urgent relief is prayed for and in light of the judgment of Supreme Court in Yamini This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:58:45 CS(COMM) 195/2026 Page 2 of 23 Manohar v. T.K.D. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SAURABH BANERJEE O R D E R % 30.05.2025 I.A. 14258/2025 (Exemption from pre-litigation mediation) 1. The plaintiff, vide the present application under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, read with Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (CPC), seeks exemption from instituting pre-litigation mediation. 2. Considering the averments made in the present application, as also since the plaintiff is seeking grant of an ex-parte ad interim injunction in an accompanying application as also appointment of a Local Commissioner, and in view of the judgment passed by the Hon’ble Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Krithi 2024 (5) SCC 815, which has been followed by a Division Bench of this Court in Chandra Kishore Chaurasia v. R. A. Perfumery Works Private Limited 2022:DHC:4454-DB, the plaintiff is exempted from instituting pre- litigation mediation. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:00 CS(COMM) 582/2025 Page 2 of 13 3. Accordingly, the present application is allowed and disposed of. I.A. 14257/2025 (Exemption from advance service) 4. The plaintiff, vide the present application under Section 151 of the CPC, seek exemption from effecting advance service upon the defendant. 5. Learned counsel for the plaintiff submits that considering the position involved, particularly since appointment of a Local Commissioner for execution of search and seizure of evidence/ counterfeit products at the premises of defendant is being sought in an accompanying application, there is a likelihood that the apprehensions of the plaintiff will become a reality in case advance service is effected and therefore, an exemption from effecting advance service is necessary. 6. For the reasons stated in the application as also taking into account the aforesaid factors and in the interest of justice, the plaintiff is granted exemption from effecting advance service upon the defendant. 7. Accordingly, the present application is allowed and disposed of. I.A. 14256/2025 (Additional documents) 8. The plaintiff, vide the present application under Order XI Rule 1(4) read with Section 151 of the CPC, seeks leave of this Court to file additional documents. 9. The plaintiff will be at liberty to file additional documents at a later stage, albeit, after initiating appropriate steps, strictly as per the provisions of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 read with Section 151 of the CPC and the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018. 10. Accordingly, the present application is allowed and disposed of. I.A. 14259/2025 (Leave to file documents in a pen-drive) 11. timeline.detail: The court disposed of several interim applications in a commercial suit for copyright infringement. Exemption from court fee was granted conditionally, pre-institution mediation was exempted due to urgency, leave to file additional documents was allowed, and statutory notice was exempted for certain proforma defendants. Summons were ordered for the infringing defendants, and directions were given for filing pleadings and obtaining subscriber information.
timeline.orderText: CS(COMM) 1306/2025 Page 1 of 18 $~44 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 1306/2025 & I.As. 30663-67/2025 DAZN LIMITED & ANR. .....Plaintiffs Through: Mr. Siddharth Chopra, Mr. Yatinder Garg, Mr. Ishi Singh and Mr. Priyansh Kohli, Advocates versus ABBONAMENTOIPTVITALIA.COM & ORS. .....Defendants Through: Mr. Abhi Udai Singh Gautam, Advocate for D-21 CORAM: HON'BLE MS. JUSTICE MANMEET PRITAM SINGH ARORA O R D E R % 08.12.2025 I.A. 30667/2025 (seeking exemption from filing court fee) 1.This is an application filed under Section 149 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (‘CPC’) seeking exemption from filing court fee at present. 2.The application is allowed subject to the court fee being deposited within one (1) week, failing which the consequences of Order VII Rule 11(b) CPC shall follow and the plaint shall stands rejected. 3.The application stands disposed of. I.A. 30666/2025 (seeking exemptionfrom pre-institution mediation) 4.This is an application filed under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 read with Section 151 CPC seeking exemption from instituting pre-litigation mediation. 5.Having regard to the fact that the present suit contemplates urgent This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:20 CS(COMM) 1306/2025 Page 2 of 18 interim relief and in light of the judgement of the Supreme Court in Yamini Manohar v. T.K.D. Keerthi 1 , exemption from the requirement of pre- institution mediation is granted to the plaintiffs. 6.Accordingly, the application stands disposed of. I.A. 30665/2025 (Under Order XI Rule 1(4) CPC) 7.This is an application seeking leave to file additional documents under Order XI Rule 1(4) of CPC [as amended by the Commercial Courts, Commercial Division and Commercial Appellate Division of High Courts Act, 2015 (‘Commercial Courts Act’)] read with Section 151 CPC, within thirty (30) days. 8.The Plaintiff, if it wishes to file additional documents, will file the same within thirty (30) days from today, and it shall do so strictly as per the provisions of the Commercial Courts Act and the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018 (‘DHC Rules’). 9.For the reasons stated in the application, the same is allowed. 10.Accordingly, the application is disposed of. I.A. 30664/2025 (seeking exemption from issuing statutory notice) 11.This is an application filed under Section 80 read with Section 151 of CPC seeking exemption from issuing notice. 12.Issue notice to defendant nos. 28, 31, 36 and 37 through all modes. 13.Keeping in view the fact that Defendant Nos. 28, 31, 36 and 37 are proforma Defendants, the exemption as sought is hereby granted. 14.The application stand disposed of. CS(COMM) 1306/2025 15.Let the plaint be registered as a suit. 1 (2024) 5 SCC 815 This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:20 CS(COMM) 1306/2025 Page 3 of 18 16.Summons be issued to defendant nos. 1 to 9, which are identified as the infringing defendants by e-mode on filing of process fee. Affidavit of service be filed within two (2) weeks. 17.The summons shall indicate that the written statement(s) must be filed within thirty (30) days from the date of receipt of the summons. The defendants shall also file affidavit(s) of admission/denial of the documents filed by the plaintiffs, failing which the written statement(s) shall not be taken on record. 18.The plaintiffs are at liberty to file replication(s) thereto within thirty (30) days after filing of the written statement(s). The replication(s) shall be accompanied by affidavit(s) of admission/denial in respect of the documents filed by defendants, failing which the replication(s) shall not be taken on record. 19.It is made clear that any unjustified denial of documents may lead to an order of costs against the concerned party. 20.Any party seeking inspection of documents may do so in accordance with the Delhi High Court (Original Side) Rules, 2018. 21.After Plaintiffs have received Basic Subscriber Information [‘BSI’] details from the proforma Defendants with respect to Defendant Nos. 1 to 9, it shall file an amended memo of parties within two (2) weeks from the receipt of the information and thereafter fresh summons will be issued to the said entities by the Joint Registrar. 22.Defendant Nos. 10 to 37 have been impleaded as ‘proforma Defendants’ in the suit for ensuring compliance of the Court’s directions. Since they are proforma Defendants, no summons is being issued to the said Defendants. The said Defendants, therefore, do not have to file written This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:20 CS(COMM) 1306/2025 Page 4 of 18 statements. Directions for compliance have been issued qua said proforma Defendants in I.A. 30663/2025. 23.Defendant No. 38 is an unknown entity-John Doe, as Plaintiff has sought relief against the unknown entities. 24.List before the learned Joint Registrar (J) on 19.01.2026. 25.List before Court on 20.05.2026. I.A. 30663/2025 (Under Order XXXIX Rule 1 and 2 CPC) 26.The Plaintiffs have filed the present Suit seeking a permanent injunction to restrain infringement of their broadcast reproduction rights by Defendant Nos. 1 to 9 and other unknown parties. 27.The learned Counsel for the Plaintiffs sets up the plaintiff’s case as follows: 27.1.The Plaintiff No. 1, DAZN Limited, is a global sports streaming and entertainment platform that offers live and on-demand/over-the-top (OTT) sports broadcasting in over 200 countries. The Plaintiff No. 1 is also an exclusive right owne

Plaintiffs may file additional documents within thirty days as per Commercial Courts Act and DHC Rules.
Defendant nos. 28, 31, 36, 37 are exempted from statutory notice.

timeline.detail: The court disposed of an application by WhatsApp LLC seeking vacation/modification of an earlier order directing disclosure of Basic Subscriber Information. The court found sufficient evidence to maintain the direction for disclosure of BSI for two phone numbers, but extended the time for compliance. The court also deleted several defendants from the suit upon compliance with earlier orders.
timeline.orderText: CS(COMM) 269/2024Page 1 of 3 $~18 *IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI +CS(COMM) 269/2024 and I.A. 7290/2024, I.A. 7292/2024 & RAZORPAY SOFTWARE PRIVATE LIMITED & ANR......Plaintiffs Through:Ms. Astha Sehgal, Advocate. versus JOHN DOE & ORS......Defendants Through:Mr. Vivek Reddy, Senior Advocate with Ms. Swati Agarwal, Mr. Mohit Singh, Mr. Vaarish Sawlani & Ms. Rithika Mathur, Advocates for D-6. Mr. Varun Pathak, Mr. Amee Rana & Ms. Sana Banyal, Advocates for D-7. Mr. Madhav Khosla, Advocate for D- 8. Ms. Nidhi Raman, CGSC with Mr. Debasish Mishra, Mr. Gokul Sharma, GP for D-9 & 10. Mr. Abhishek Anand, Advocate for D-11. Mr. K.G. Gopalakrishnan, Ms. Nisha Mohandas & Mr. Kunwar Raj Singh, Advocates. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AMIT BANSAL O R D E R %28.04.2025 I.A. 38259/2024 (under Order XXXIX Rule 4 of CPC) 1.This application has been filed on behalf of the defendant no. 6/ WhatsApp LLC, seeking vacation/ modification of paragraph no. 20.4 of the ex-parteinterim order passed by this Court on 2 nd April, 2024. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:54 CS(COMM) 269/2024Page 2 of 3 2.The relevant extract of the order of which the modification is sought, is set out below: “20.4. Defendant No. 6, WhatsApp LLC, is directed to file within four weeks from today, the Basic Subscriber Information relating to the WhatsApp accounts of the phone numbers +91-6398023058 and +91- 8103258963, as is available with them, in a sealed cover. A copy of the said documents shall also be provided to counsel for Plaintiffs, which shall be strictly used by them for the purpose of investigation and identification of the perpetrators.” 3.It is stated that insofar as the numbers which are the subject matter of the directions passed in paragraph no. 20.4 are concerned, the plaintiffs have not provided requisite evidence in support of its allegations. 4.Counsel for the plaintiffs has taken me to the evidence placed on record. 5.Insofar as the number +91 8103258963 is concerned, attention of the Court has been drawn to the complaint filed by the alleged victim (page no. 200 of the plaintiffs’ documents filed with the plaint). 6.In the complaint, the victim has clearly stated that the owner of the aforesaid number told the victim that he/ she worked in the plaintiff company, and called upon the victim to part with money. 7.Insofar as the number +91-6398023058 is concerned, attention of this Court has been drawn to page nos. 171 and 166 of the plaintiffs’ documents filed with the plaint. 8.In the screenshots of the WhatsApp messages filed therein, at page no. 171 of the plaintiffs’ documents, a reference has been given to a Telegram channelhttps://t.me/freestyle4425, which as per the plaintiff has been used to defraud victims. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:54 CS(COMM) 269/2024Page 3 of 3 9.A reference to page no. 166 would show that the owner of the said number had introduced himself as the manager of the plaintiff company, and has included the complainant in the aforesaid Telegram group. 10.In my considered view, the aforesaid evidence is sufficient at this stage, at least for the purposes of disclosure of the Basic Subscriber Information (‘BSI’) of the owners of the said numbers. 10.1.The BSI details shall be filed by the applicant/defendant no.6 in a sealed cover, before this Court, within two (2) weeks from today. 10.2.A copy of the BSI information would also be provided to the counsel for the plaintiffs. 11.It is made clear that the plaintiffs shall use the said information only for the purposes of the present suit. 12.In view of the order passed above, the application is accordingly disposed of. CS(COMM) 269/2024 13.Since the defendants no. 3, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 15 and 18 have complied with the order dated 2 nd April, 2024, counsel for the plaintiffs submits that she has no difficulty, if the aforesaid defendants are deleted from the array of parties. 13.1.It is ordered accordingly. 14.Amended memo of parties be filed within one (1) week. 15.List on 10 th September, 2025. AMIT BANSAL, J APRIL 28, 2025/at This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:54

Defendants 3, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 15, 18 deleted from array of parties upon compliance

timeline.detail: The court considered multiple applications in a suit alleging fraud by Defendant No. 1 through false association with plaintiffs. Exemption from advance service on Defendant No. 1 was granted due to the nature of the fraud. Other applications regarding additional documents, exemption from filing originals, and notice under Section 80 CPC were disposed of. Summons were issued to defendants, and the matter was listed for further proceedings.
timeline.orderText: CS(COMM) 269/2024 Page 1 of 16 $~45 * IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI + CS(COMM) 269/2024 RAZORPAY SOFTWARE PRIVATE LIMITED & ANR..... Plaintiff Through: Ms. Sneha Jain, Advocate with Mr. Yatinder Garg, Mr. Sanidhya Maheshwari and Ms. Rimjhim Tiwari, Advocates. versus JOHN DOE & ORS. ..... Defendants Through: Ms. Anushka Sharda, Advocate with Ms. Madhav Khosla and Ms. Smriti Nair, Advocates for D-8. Ms. Nidhi Raman, CGSC with Mr. Zubin Singh, Advocate for Defendant Nos. 9 & 10. CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANJEEV NARULA O R D E R % 02.04.2024 I.A. 7294/2024 (seeking exemption from serving Defendant No. 1 in advance) 1. The Plaintiffs allege that Defendant No. 1 is perpetrating fraud on the general public by creating a false association with them, resulting in grave financial losses to the public. Considering the nature of controversy involved in the present suit as well as the peculiar facts and circumstances of the case, exemption from effecting advance service on Defendant No. 1 is allowed. 2. Application is disposed of. This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:55 CS(COMM) 269/2024 Page 2 of 16 I.A. 7291/2024 (seeking leave to file additional documents) 3. This is an application seeking leave to file additional documents under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. 4. Plaintiffs, if they wish to file additional documents at a later stage, shall do so strictly as per the provisions of the said Act. 5. Accordingly, the application stands disposed of. I.A. 7293/2024 (seeking exemption from filing original, clear copies, prayer copies and documents with proper margins) 6. Exemption is granted, subject to all just exceptions. 7. The Plaintiffs shall file legible and clearer copies of exempted documents, compliant with practice rules, before the next date of hearing. 8. Accordingly, the application stands disposed of. I.A. 7292/2024 (under Section 80 of CPC for exemption from issuing notice to Defendant No. 9 and 10) 9. Issue notice. Ms. Nidhi Raman, CGSC for Defendants No. 9 and 10 accepts notice. Reply, if any, be filed within four weeks from today. Rejoinder thereto, if any, be filed within two weeks’ thereafter. 10. Re-notify on 03 rd September, 2024. CS(COMM) 269/2024 11. Let the plaint be registered as a suit. 12. Issue summons. Counsel for Defendants mentioned in the appearance above, accept notice on behalf of their respective Defendants. Written statement(s) shall be filed on behalf of the said Defendants within 30 days from today. Upon filing of process fee, issue summons to the remaining Defendants, by all permissible modes. Summons shall state that the written This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:55 CS(COMM) 269/2024 Page 3 of 16 statement(s) shall be filed by the Defendants within 30 days from the date of receipt of summons. 13. Along with the written statement(s), the Defendants shall also file affidavit(s) of admission/denial of the documents of the Plaintiff, without which the written statement(s) shall not be taken on record. 14. Liberty is given to the Plaintiffs to file replication(s) within 15 days of the receipt of the written statement(s). Along with the replication(s), if any, filed by the Plaintiffs, affidavit(s) of admission/denial of documents of the Defendants, be filed by the Plaintiffs, without which the replication(s) shall not be taken on record. If any of the parties wish to seek inspection of any documents, the same shall be sought and given within the timelines. 15. List before the Joint Registrar for marking of exhibits on 02 nd July, 2024. It is made clear that any party unjustifiably denying documents would be liable to be burdened with costs. 16. List before Court for framing of issues thereafter. I.A. 7290/2024 (u/Order XXXIX Rules 1 & 2, r/w Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908) 17. Ms. Sneha Jain, counsel for Plaintiff, presents the following facts and contentions for an ad-interim ex-parte order of injunction against Defendant No. 1: 17.1. The Plaintiffs are renowned payment gateway service providers, specializing in developing application programme interface for a variety of financial products and other digital financial services, such as payment and payroll processing. Plaintiff No. 1, Razorpay Software Private Limited, was incorporated in the year 2013, and Plaintiff No. 2, its group company, was This is a digitally signed order. The authenticity of the order can be re-verified from Delhi High Court Order Portal by scanning the QR code shown above. The Order is downloaded from the DHC Server on 29/04/2026 at 13:59:55 CS(COMM) 269/2024 Page 4 of 16 established in 2014. Since 2013, the Plaintiffs have been offering a fast, affordable, and secure way for end-to-end online payments, under the trademark “RAZORPAY” since 2013. 17.2. The “RAZORPAY” product profile entails a portfolio of well- recognized technological products, such as, payment gateway, links and pages and payroll. 17.3. Plaintiff No. 2 has obtained registrations for the trademarks “RAZORPAY”, “RAZORPAY X”, “RAZORPAY CAPITAL”, “”, “”, “”, “” and several other formative variants thereof, 1 in classes 9, 35, 36, 38 and 42, inter alia in respect of electronic payment and financial transaction services, design and development of computer hardware, business administration, and telecommunications. Particulars of registration have been set put in paragraph No. 23 of the plaint. 17.4. Plaintiffs jointly operate the domain name “www.razorpay.com”, which has been registered in the name of Plaintiff No. 2. Along with this website, the Plaintiffs have also developed a mobile application. Both the website and the mobile applicati

The plaintiff agreed to furnish the copy during the day. The court directed the defendant to file a reply within four weeks and the plaintiff to file a rejoinder within four weeks thereafter, and listed the matter for further hearing on 16.04.2026.
